the-executives-ai-reality-check.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to evaluate whether a specific business process in your organization could benefit from AI. Include the process details and what 'better' would look like.
the-executives-ai-reality-check.placeholder: Evaluate whether AI could improve our...
the-executives-ai-reality-check.hint: We spend 40 hours/week manually reviewing customer support tickets to route them to the right department. Evaluate whether AI could automate this. Consider: accuracy requirements (we can't misroute medical inquiries), current volume (2,000 tickets/day), and our team of 8 human reviewers. What's realistic?
the-executives-ai-reality-check.goodExample: Our sales team manually qualifies 500 inbound leads per week by reading form submissions and scoring them 1-5. This takes 3 reps about 15 hours combined. Evaluate whether AI could automate initial lead scoring. What accuracy rate would we need to trust it? What's the risk if it gets it wrong?
the-executives-ai-reality-check.successMessage: Great executive thinking — you framed the problem with real numbers, constraints, and risk considerations. That's how you evaluate AI opportunities without the hype.

the-executives-ai-reality-check.instruction: Rewrite this vague AI initiative proposal to make it decision-ready: 'We should use AI to improve customer experience.'
the-executives-ai-reality-check.hint: Proposal: Deploy an AI-powered chatbot to handle our top 20 FAQ queries (which make up 60% of support volume). Expected outcome: reduce average response time from 4 hours to under 2 minutes for these queries. Investment: ~$2K/month. Success metric: maintain 90%+ customer satisfaction while reducing support ticket volume by 40% in 90 days.
the-executives-ai-reality-check.badExample: We should use AI to improve customer experience
the-executives-ai-reality-check.goodExample: Proposal: Implement AI-assisted email drafting for our 12-person customer success team. Tool: Claude for Business ($30/user/month = $360/month). Goal: reduce average email composition time from 8 minutes to 3 minutes. Pilot: 4-week trial with 4 team members. Success metric: same or better CSAT scores with 40% faster response times.
the-executives-ai-reality-check.successMessage: Now that's a proposal a board can actually vote on. Specific tool, specific cost, specific metric, specific timeline. That's how you cut through AI hype.

ai-strategy-without-the-jargon.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to help you create a plain-language AI strategy summary for your leadership team. Specify your industry, company size, and current AI maturity.
ai-strategy-without-the-jargon.placeholder: Create an AI strategy brief for...
ai-strategy-without-the-jargon.hint: Create a 1-page AI strategy brief for the leadership team of a 200-person accounting firm. We currently use zero AI tools. Budget for AI exploration: $50K this year. Our biggest pain points: document review (800 hours/quarter), client email management, and report generation. Write it in plain English — no jargon. Include 3 recommended starting points ranked by effort vs. impact.
ai-strategy-without-the-jargon.goodExample: Write a 1-page AI strategy memo for the CEO of a 50-person marketing agency. We use Canva and HubSpot but no AI tools. Top 3 bottlenecks: content creation (takes 6 hours per blog post), social media scheduling, and competitive analysis. Budget: $2K/month. Write it so a non-technical CEO can read it in 5 minutes and make a decision.
ai-strategy-without-the-jargon.successMessage: A strategy brief that a non-technical executive can read and act on — that's the bridge between AI potential and AI adoption.

ai-strategy-without-the-jargon.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to identify the 3 highest-impact AI use cases for a specific department. Include current workflow details and constraints.
ai-strategy-without-the-jargon.placeholder: Identify the top 3 AI opportunities for our...
ai-strategy-without-the-jargon.hint: Identify the top 3 AI opportunities for our HR department (team of 6). Current bottlenecks: screening 300+ resumes per role, writing consistent job descriptions across 15 departments, and scheduling interviews across 3 time zones. Constraints: we must comply with EU AI Act hiring regulations. Rank by: quickest to implement with lowest risk.
ai-strategy-without-the-jargon.badExample: Where should we use AI in our company?
ai-strategy-without-the-jargon.goodExample: Identify the top 3 AI opportunities for our HR department (team of 6). Current bottlenecks: screening 300+ resumes per open role, writing consistent job descriptions across 15 departments, and scheduling interviews across 3 time zones. Constraints: must comply with EU AI Act hiring regulations. Rank by: quickest to implement with lowest risk first.
ai-strategy-without-the-jargon.successMessage: Identifying high-impact, low-risk starting points is the executive superpower. You don't need to transform everything — just start where it matters most.

measuring-ai-roi.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to build an ROI framework for a specific AI initiative. Include costs, expected benefits, and how to measure success.
measuring-ai-roi.hint: Build a simple ROI model for deploying AI-powered document summarization for our legal team (8 lawyers). Current state: each lawyer spends 10 hours/week reading and summarizing contracts. Tool cost: $500/month. Lawyer hourly rate: $250. Calculate: monthly time saved, dollar value, breakeven point, and 6-month projected ROI. Include risks that could reduce the ROI.
measuring-ai-roi.goodExample: Create an ROI framework for implementing AI chatbot support for our SaaS product (10,000 users, 200 tickets/day). Costs: $800/month platform + 40 hours setup. Current support cost: 3 FTEs at $55K each. Measure: ticket deflection rate, resolution time, CSAT change, and cost per resolved ticket. Show me the math for 3 scenarios: conservative (20% deflection), moderate (40%), optimistic (60%).
measuring-ai-roi.successMessage: An ROI model with scenarios, real numbers, and risk factors — this is how executives make AI investment decisions with confidence.

measuring-ai-roi.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to create a dashboard of KPIs for tracking an AI initiative's performance after launch.
measuring-ai-roi.hint: Design a KPI dashboard for tracking our AI-powered sales email assistant after launch. We need to measure: adoption (are reps actually using it?), quality (are AI-drafted emails performing as well as human ones?), and efficiency (time saved per rep per day). Include leading indicators we can check weekly and lagging indicators for the quarterly review.
measuring-ai-roi.badExample: How do I track if our AI tool is working?
measuring-ai-roi.goodExample: Design a KPI dashboard for tracking our AI-powered sales email assistant after launch. Measure across 3 dimensions: adoption (% of reps using it daily, prompts per rep per week), quality (open rates and reply rates of AI-drafted vs. human-drafted emails), and efficiency (minutes saved per rep per day). Include leading indicators for weekly check-ins and lagging indicators for the quarterly board review.
measuring-ai-roi.successMessage: KPIs turn AI experiments into managed initiatives. If you can't measure it, you can't manage it — and you definitely can't justify scaling it.

ai-risk-and-governance.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to create a risk assessment for deploying AI in a specific business function. Include data sensitivity, regulatory requirements, and stakeholder concerns.
ai-risk-and-governance.placeholder: Assess the risks of using AI for...
ai-risk-and-governance.hint: Assess the risks of deploying AI to assist with medical insurance claims processing. Consider: HIPAA compliance, error rates in claim categorization, bias in approval/denial patterns, staff displacement concerns (we have 25 claims processors), and reputational risk if AI denies a valid claim. Categorize risks as high/medium/low and suggest a mitigation strategy for each.
ai-risk-and-governance.badExample: What are the risks of AI?
ai-risk-and-governance.goodExample: Create a risk assessment for using AI to generate personalized investment recommendations for our wealth management clients. Cover: SEC/FINRA regulatory requirements, fiduciary liability if AI gives bad advice, data privacy (we handle PII and financial data), model bias toward certain demographics, and client trust impact. Rate each risk and propose controls.
ai-risk-and-governance.successMessage: A structured risk assessment with specific regulatory context and mitigation plans — this is responsible AI governance in action.

ai-risk-and-governance.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to draft an AI usage policy for your organization. Include scope, approved uses, prohibited uses, and accountability.
ai-risk-and-governance.placeholder: Draft an AI usage policy for...
ai-risk-and-governance.hint: Draft a 1-page AI usage policy for a 100-person marketing agency. Cover: approved tools (ChatGPT, Claude, Midjourney), approved uses (brainstorming, first drafts, research), prohibited uses (final client deliverables without human review, inputting client confidential data), who approves new AI tools, and how we disclose AI use to clients. Keep it practical, not legalistic.
ai-risk-and-governance.badExample: Write an AI policy for us
ai-risk-and-governance.goodExample: Draft a 1-page AI usage policy for a 100-person marketing agency. Cover: approved tools (ChatGPT, Claude, Midjourney), approved uses (brainstorming, first drafts, internal research), prohibited uses (final client deliverables without human review, inputting client confidential data), who approves new AI tools, and how we disclose AI use to clients. Keep it practical and plain-English, not legalistic.
ai-risk-and-governance.successMessage: A clear, practical AI policy protects your organization while empowering your team to use AI confidently. Well structured.

ai-vendor-selection-playbook.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to create a vendor evaluation scorecard for comparing AI tools for a specific use case.
ai-vendor-selection-playbook.hint: Create a vendor evaluation scorecard for comparing 3 AI writing assistants for our content team of 12 writers. Score on: output quality for long-form content, integration with Google Workspace, data privacy (we handle client info), per-seat pricing under $50/month, learning curve (team is non-technical), and customer support responsiveness. Weight the criteria by importance to us.
ai-vendor-selection-playbook.goodExample: Build a weighted evaluation scorecard for choosing an AI customer support platform. Must-haves: multilingual (English, Spanish, French), Salesforce integration, SOC 2 compliance. Nice-to-haves: sentiment analysis, custom training on our knowledge base. Compare against: cost per resolution, implementation time, and scalability from 100 to 1,000 tickets/day. Weight must-haves at 2x.
ai-vendor-selection-playbook.successMessage: A weighted scorecard turns subjective vendor opinions into objective, defensible decisions. This is how enterprises buy AI responsibly.

ai-vendor-selection-playbook.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to generate the key questions you should ask an AI vendor during a sales demo.
ai-vendor-selection-playbook.hint: Generate 10 critical questions to ask during a demo of an AI-powered recruitment platform. Focus on: how they handle bias in screening, what happens to candidate data after the contract ends, how their model is trained (and on what data), their uptime SLA, and how they handle edge cases the AI can't process. I want questions that expose real capabilities, not marketing claims.
ai-vendor-selection-playbook.badExample: What questions should I ask an AI vendor?
ai-vendor-selection-playbook.goodExample: Generate 10 critical questions to ask during a demo of an AI-powered recruitment platform. Focus on: how they handle bias in resume screening, what happens to candidate data after the contract ends, how their model is trained (and on what data), their uptime SLA, and how they handle edge cases the AI can't process. I want questions that expose real capabilities vs. marketing claims.
ai-vendor-selection-playbook.successMessage: The right questions during a demo separate AI that works from AI that's just a good pitch deck. These will save you from expensive mistakes.

the-ai-ready-organization.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to assess your organization's AI readiness across people, process, data, and technology dimensions.
the-ai-ready-organization.hint: Assess our organization's AI readiness. Context: 300-person retail company, data stored in 4 disconnected systems (POS, CRM, inventory, e-commerce), IT team of 5, no data scientist, leadership is enthusiastic but the frontline staff is skeptical. Evaluate across: data quality and accessibility, technical infrastructure, team skills and culture, leadership alignment. Give us a readiness score and a 90-day preparation plan.
the-ai-ready-organization.goodExample: Evaluate our AI readiness for a 60-person law firm. Our data: case files in 3 formats (PDF, Word, paper scans), no centralized document management, and no existing automation. Our people: partners are curious but cautious, associates are eager, IT is one person. Score us 1-5 on: data readiness, technical infrastructure, team capability, leadership commitment, and change management capacity. Identify the top 3 gaps to close first.
the-ai-ready-organization.successMessage: An honest readiness assessment prevents the #1 AI failure: deploying technology into an organization that isn't prepared to use it.

the-ai-ready-organization.instruction: In 3-5 sentences, describe what 'AI-ready culture' looks like in your organization. What needs to change in how your team thinks about AI?
the-ai-ready-organization.placeholder: An AI-ready culture in our org means...
the-ai-ready-organization.hint: An AI-ready culture means every team member sees AI as a power tool, not a threat. It means managers measure output quality, not hours at desk. It means we have psychological safety to experiment and fail with AI tools. And it means leadership uses AI themselves — not just mandates it for others.
the-ai-ready-organization.badExample: We need to adopt AI and everyone should be excited about it.
the-ai-ready-organization.goodExample: An AI-ready culture means every team member sees AI as a power tool, not a threat to their job. It means managers measure output quality and impact, not hours at a desk. It means we have psychological safety to experiment with AI tools and fail without blame. And it means leadership uses AI themselves visibly — not just mandating it for others while avoiding it personally.
the-ai-ready-organization.successMessage: Culture eats strategy for breakfast — and it eats AI strategy for every meal. Getting this right determines whether your AI investments pay off.

building-your-ai-team.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to help you design the structure of an AI team or center of excellence for your organization size and goals.
building-your-ai-team.placeholder: Design an AI team structure for...
building-your-ai-team.hint: Design an AI center of excellence for a 500-person healthcare company. Budget: 2 new hires + upskilling existing staff. We need someone to: evaluate AI tools, train departments, ensure HIPAA compliance, and manage vendor relationships. Should this report to IT, operations, or the C-suite? Suggest roles, reporting structure, and a 6-month roadmap.
building-your-ai-team.goodExample: Help me plan an AI function for a 150-person financial services firm. We can't hire a full data science team. Options I'm considering: (1) upskill 2 existing analysts, (2) hire 1 AI lead + use consultants, (3) outsource entirely. Compare these options on: cost, speed to value, knowledge retention, and risk. Recommend the best fit for our size.
building-your-ai-team.successMessage: Right-sizing your AI team to your organization prevents both under-investing and over-spending. Practical structure over hype.

leading-ai-transformation.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to help you create a change management communication plan for rolling out AI tools to a skeptical team.
leading-ai-transformation.hint: Create a 4-week internal communication plan for rolling out AI writing assistants to our 40-person content team. Half the team is excited, half is worried about job security. Week by week: what message to send, who delivers it, and what format (email, town hall, hands-on demo). Address the job security fear directly with honesty — no corporate platitudes.
leading-ai-transformation.goodExample: Design a change management plan for introducing AI-assisted customer service to a team of 20 support agents. Key concerns: 'will this replace us?' and 'I'm not technical enough.' Plan should include: a kickoff message from me (the VP), a hands-on workshop, a buddy system for adoption, and weekly check-ins for the first month. Tone: honest and empathetic, not corporate.
leading-ai-transformation.successMessage: Leading AI transformation is 20% technology and 80% people. A thoughtful communication plan is the difference between adoption and resistance.

leading-ai-transformation.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to help you create an executive briefing that translates a technical AI concept into business language.
leading-ai-transformation.placeholder: Translate this AI concept for executives...
leading-ai-transformation.hint: Explain RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) to a board of directors who have no technical background. Use a business analogy they'll understand. Then explain why it matters for our company's customer support AI — specifically how it reduces hallucinations and keeps responses accurate to our product documentation. Under 200 words. No jargon.
leading-ai-transformation.goodExample: Explain RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) to a board of directors with no technical background. Use a business analogy they'll understand — like a research analyst who checks the company handbook before answering instead of guessing. Then explain why it matters for our customer support AI: how it reduces made-up answers and keeps responses accurate to our actual product documentation. Under 200 words. No jargon.
leading-ai-transformation.successMessage: The ability to translate technical AI concepts into business value is the most important executive AI skill. Well done.

ai-for-your-industry.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to map the AI landscape for your specific industry. Include what competitors are doing and where the biggest opportunities are.
ai-for-your-industry.hint: Map the AI landscape for mid-size commercial real estate firms (50-200 employees). Cover: what the top firms are doing with AI right now, which AI tools are purpose-built for CRE, where the biggest efficiency gains are (deal sourcing, property valuation, tenant communication, lease analysis), and what's still too immature to bet on. Focus on 2025-2026 realities, not 5-year predictions.
ai-for-your-industry.badExample: How is AI used in my industry?
ai-for-your-industry.goodExample: Analyze the AI competitive landscape for independent insurance agencies (10-50 agents). What are the big carriers implementing that will affect us? Which AI tools can help us compete with larger agencies? Focus on: claims processing, policy comparison, client communication, and risk assessment. Separate what's available now from what's still vaporware.
ai-for-your-industry.successMessage: Industry-specific AI intelligence is worth more than generic AI knowledge. You now know how to get strategic insights tailored to your exact competitive position.
